chunks.title: Course Information
chunks.content:
Section 550 (58970)
Computational Data Science
Spring 2026 - College Station

Catalog Description
Computational Data Science. (3-0). Credit 3. Computational practice of data science through a sequence of interactive modules that provides an integrated hands-on approach to its methods, tools, applications and supporting technologies including high performance and cloud computing platforms. Prerequisites: Grade of C or better in ENGR 102, CSCE 110, CSCE 111, or CSCE 206; grade of C or better in MATH 251, MATH 253, or STAT 211; junior or senior classification. Cross-listed with ECEN 360 and STAT 315.

Special Course Designation
None

chunks.title: Course Prerequisites
chunks.content: Prerequisite/Corequisite(s): Grade of C or better in ENGR 102, CSCE 110, CSCE 111, or CSCE 206; grade of C or better in MATH 251, MATH 253, or STAT 211; junior or senior classification. Cross Listing: ECEN 360 and STAT 315.

chunks.title: Course Learning Outcomes
chunks.content:
Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:
• Develop the basic components of data science programming.
• Learn the concepts related to data science and Python.
• Evaluate composition and functionality of the functions, sequence, lists, tuples, and strings.
• Use data science programming libraries and analyze Python libraries and their uses.
• Apply best practices for developing and verifying effective data science programming practices.
• Develop techniques to improve programming practices.

chunks.title: Textbook and/or Resource Materials
chunks.content:
This material Is: Required
Intro to Python for Computer Science and Data Science: Learning to Program with AI, Big Data and The Cloud
Subtitle: Learning to Program with AI, Big Data and The Cloud
ISBN: 9780135404676
Authors: Paul Deitel, Harvey Deitel

This material Is: Recommended
Deep Learning
ISBN: 9780262035613
Authors: Ian Goodfellow, Yoshua Bengio, Aaron Courville
Available free online at: https://www.deeplearningbook.org/

Online Resources:
• Canvas: All course materials, assignments, and announcements will be posted on Canvas
• Python Documentation: https://docs.python.org/3/
• NumPy Documentation: https://numpy.org/doc/
• PyTorch Documentation: https://pytorch.org/docs/

Software Requirements:
• Python 3.x (Anaconda distribution recommended)
• Jupyter Notebook or JupyterLab
• Required packages: NumPy, Pandas, Matplotlib, Scikit-learn, PyTorch

chunks.title: Grading Policy
chunks.content:
Grading Components:
- Mid-term Exam: 20%
- Final Exam: 20%
- Quizzes: 10%
- Lab Assignments: 50%
- Total: 100%
Grading Scale: A ≥ 90% > B ≥ 80% > C ≥ 70% > D ≥ 60% > F
We do curve in this class. Due dates will be posted on Canvas and are due 11:59 p.m., unless announced otherwise.

chunks.title: Late Work Policy
chunks.content:
Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy (Student Rule 7).

Course Specific Late Work Policy
The request for deadline extensions should be submitted at least two business days (not counting weekends) before the due date. "Unapproved" late assignments will receive a grade of zero.
Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy (Student Rule 7).

chunks.title: AI Statement
chunks.content:
With the rise of artificial intelligence (AI) technologies, programming and problem-solving practices are evolving. While these tools can assist with brainstorming, debugging, or exploring alternative approaches, they do not replace the essential skills of designing, writing, and testing your own code. Nor do they substitute for the critical thinking required to analyze problems, evaluate solutions, and apply course concepts independently.

In this course, you are permitted to use generative AI tools (e.g., ChatGPT, GitHub Copilot, Google Gemini) to support your learning, under the following conditions:
• AI may be used as a supplement, not a substitute. You may consult AI for hints, debugging suggestions, or explanations, but you are expected to write and understand your own code.
• You must cite AI assistance. If AI tools inform your work (e.g., generating a code snippet, suggesting an algorithm, or helping you debug), you must clearly document this in comments within your code (e.g., # AI tool (ChatGPT) suggested this loop structure ).
• AI should not complete your assignments for you. Submitting work primarily written by AI is considered academic dishonesty. You are responsible for understanding and being able to explain every line of code you submit.
• Limit reliance. No more than 30% of an assignment's code may be directly AI-generated. The majority of the work must reflect your own effort and understanding.

Important reminders:
• Generative AI may produce incorrect or inefficient code. Always test and verify any AI-assisted solution before using it.
• AI tools may generate code that does not follow best practices or course requirements. You are responsible for ensuring your work meets assignment expectations.
• Misrepresentation of AI-generated work as your own will be reported to the Aggie Honor System Office. The minimum penalties include:
• A grade of 0 on the assignment
• A grade of F in the course
• Honor Violation Probation
